prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I have everything I need. Here is the complete diagnosis.

---

## Diagnosis

The content you rewrote is visible on disk but **invisible on the live site**. No errors, no warnings, no broken build — the site just keeps serving the old text. This is a textbook case of **EAP-087** (Modifying a Content Seeding Route Without Calling the Endpoint), and it has happened on this exact project with this exact file before — see **ENG-149** in the feedback log.

---

## Root Cause

### The Two-Layer Architecture You Have to Respect

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 1 — Source of record (what you edited)           │
│  src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts            │
│  Defines: BLURB_BODY, CONTENT_BLOCKS, scope statement   │
│  This file is code — it is NOT content on the website.  │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                         │  HTTP POST (must be explicitly called)
                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 2 — Source of truth (what the site reads)        │
│  Payload CMS database (MongoDB / SQLite)                 │
│  Project document: slug "etro-framework"                 │
│  Fields: blurb, scope, content blocks (Lexical JSON)    │
│  THIS is what gets rendered at runtime.                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

`route.ts` is a **seeding endpoint** — it contains the intended values and, when called via HTTP, writes them into the CMS database. **The file itself has no side effects.** Saving it does not push anything to the database. The website reads from the database, not from the source file. Your new content exists in the TypeScript file and nowhere else.

This is the same split-brain state that caused **ENG-149** ("ETRO essay content rewrite not visible on website — seeding endpoint never called") and **ENG-204** ("Elan image placeholder section kept reappearing after CMS deletion — seeding route still defined the old block, overwrote CMS on every call"). Both incidents trace to **EAP-087**.

A secondary principle is also involved: **EAP-004** (Modifying Source of Truth Without Updating Consumers) — except here the relationship is inverted. The route file is the *intended* source of truth for a seed, but the *actual* runtime source of truth is the database. Editing the seed template without running it leaves the two permanently out of sync.

---

## Fix

### Immediate: call the endpoint to push your content to the CMS

The route is a standard Next.js API route. Call it from localhost while the dev server is running:

```bash
# 1. Confirm you're on dev, not main
git branch --show-current   # should print: dev

# 2. Start the dev server if not already running
# (check docs/port-registry.md for the correct port — typically 4000)

# 3. Trigger the seed
curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro
# Expect: { "success": true } or similar confirmation body

# 4. Verify the CMS received it — open the admin panel
open http://localhost:4000/admin
# Navigate to: Projects → etro-framework
# Confirm the blurb, scope, and all five section bodies match your route.ts

# 5. Verify the frontend page renders the new content
open http://localhost:4000/essays/etro-framework
# or wherever the ETRO page lives — check that the new opening line
# "Your whole approach is wrong." is visible
```

If you've already deployed to Vercel without seeding locally first, you also need to call the production endpoint once (with your Payload secret header if the route is gated):

```bash
curl -X POST https://yilangao.com/api/update-etro \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $PAYLOAD_SECRET"
```

> ⚠️ **Check the route for auth guards first.** Open `route.ts` and look for any `request.headers.get('Authorization')` check before running the curl.

---

### Workflow Going Forward: The Three-Step Rule for Content Work

Every content session that touches a seeding route must follow this sequence — no exceptions:

```
Step 1 — EDIT    Edit the route.ts constants (BLURB_BODY, CONTENT_BLOCKS, etc.)
Step 2 — SEED    curl -X POST localhost:{port}/api/update-{slug}  ← THE STEP ALWAYS MISSED
Step 3 — VERIFY  Open the page in a browser. Confirm the new first line is visible.
```

**Guardrail Engineering-25** (from `AGENTS.md`) codifies this:
> *ALWAYS call the content seeding endpoint after modifying any `update-*/route.ts` file. The file edit is not the content change — the HTTP call is.*

This guardrail exists precisely because the file-save feels complete. It isn't. The database write is the real commit.

---

### Structural Improvement: Add a Comment to the Route File Itself

To make the two-step requirement impossible to miss, add a seeding instruction directly at the top of the file. Future sessions — human or agent — will see it immediately:

```ts
// src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts
//
// ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
// │  SEEDING ROUTE — editing this file does NOT update the website. │
// │  After every edit, you MUST call:                               │
// │    curl -X POST http://localhost:{port}/api/update-etro         │
// │  Then verify the page in the browser. See EAP-087.             │
// └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Principle

**EAP-087 / Guardrail Engineering-25:** A content seeding route is a script that writes to a database — not a config file that the application reads. The visible artifact is the database record, not the TypeScript constant. Editing the route file changes the *intention*; calling the endpoint changes the *reality*. These are two separate acts, and the second one is the one that matters.

Any workflow that treats "file saved" as "content updated" is broken by design. The test for whether content work is complete is not whether the file compiles — it's whether the new first sentence appears in the browser.
